stem: A drawer contains socks of \( [[k]] \) different colors, with plenty of each color. What is the minimum number of socks you must pick (without looking) to be GUARANTEED at least \( [[m]] \) socks of the same color?
solution: In the worst case you pick \( [[m]]-1 \) socks of EACH of the \( [[k]] \) colors before a repeat is forced; one more sock then guarantees \( [[m]] \) of some color: \( [[k]]([[m]]-1)+1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This assumes you need a full set of \( m \) in every color, not just one color.
This just adds the two quantities instead of applying the pigeonhole worst-case count.
This forgets the final \( +1 \) sock that actually forces the guarantee.
This adds one to the wrong (over-multiplied) quantity.
This is one more than the correct minimum.
This is one less than the correct minimum.

stem: \( [[n]] \) balls are distributed among \( [[k]] \) boxes (in any way). What is the largest number \( N \) such that, no matter how the balls are distributed, SOME box is guaranteed to contain at least \( N \) balls?
solution: By the pigeonhole principle, some box must contain at least \( \lceil n/k \rceil = \lceil [[n]]/[[k]] \rceil = [[answer]] \) balls (an even split rounded up).
distractor_rationales: This rounds DOWN instead of up, which is not guaranteed to hold in every distribution.
This uses the un-rounded average, which need not be an integer.
This overshoots the guaranteed minimum by one.
This just reports the number of boxes, unrelated to the guaranteed count.
This is one more than the correct value.
This is one less than the correct value.

stem: A drawer contains \( [[c1]] \) red socks, \( [[c2]] \) blue socks, and \( [[c3]] \) green socks. What is the minimum number of socks you must draw (without looking) to be GUARANTEED that at least one of them is RED?
solution: In the worst case you draw every non-red sock first (\( [[c2]]+[[c3]] \) of them); the very next sock must be red: \( [[c2]]+[[c3]]+1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This forgets the extra draw that actually forces a red sock.
This uses the count of red socks instead of the count of non-red socks.
This is an unnecessarily convoluted way of writing the wrong count, still missing the actual worst case.
This just reports the number of red socks available, not a guarantee count.
This is one more than the correct minimum.
This is one less than the correct minimum.

stem: Consider the integers from 1 to \( [[twon]] \), inclusive. What is the minimum number of these integers you must select to GUARANTEE that at least two of the selected integers sum to \( [[target]] \)?
solution: Pair up the integers as \( \{1,[[twon]]\}, \{2,[[twon]]-1\}, \ldots \) — there are \( [[n]] \) such pairs, each summing to \( [[target]] \). Picking one integer from every pair avoids a match, so you can pick \( [[n]] \) integers safely; one more forces two from the same pair: \( [[n]]+1 = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is the number of pairs itself, without the extra pick that forces a match.
This is the total number of integers available, far more than actually needed.
This overshoots the guaranteed minimum by one.
This is exactly one short of forcing a matching pair.
This is one more than the correct minimum.
This is one less than the correct minimum.
